coredata.dc:title: Phosphorus, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for real-time measurement of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species inside macrophages
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Yunqian
Yu, Bin
Yang, Wen
Zhang, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: Phosphorus and nitrogen doped carbon dots (PN-CDs) were conveniently prepared by carbonization of adenosine-5′-triphosphate using a hydrothermal treatment. The PN-CDs with P/C atomic ratio of ca. 9.2/100 emit blue luminescence with high quantum yields of up to 23.5%. The PN-CDs were used as a novel sensing platform for live cell imaging of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS), including ClO−, ONOO−, and NO in macrophages. The nanosensor design is based on our new finding that the strong fluorescence of the PN-CDs can be sensitively and selectively quenched by ROS and RNS both in vitro and in vivo. These results reveal that the PN-CDs can serve as a sensitive sensor for rapid imaging of ROS and RNS signaling with high selectivity and contrast.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cell imaging
Adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP)
Fluorescent probe
Reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS)
Real-time measurement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of ROS/RNS specificity and sensitivity
In vitro studies of PN-CDs for ROS and RNS detection
Live cell imaging of ROS and RNS with PN-CDs in macrophages

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21275018 and 21203008 ), Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS) ( 20140121 ), Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (RFDP) (No. 20121101110049 ) and the 111 Project ( B07012 ) for financial support. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the PN-CDs. (b) The high-resolution TEM image of the PN-CDs. (c) Survey XPS spectrum of the PN-CDs. (d) High-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of the PN-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra (with progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 260 to 420nm). The insets show the digital photographs of the PN-CDs solution in air using a UV lamp (365nm). (b) Fluorescence decay curve of the PN-CDs (red line) with excitation at 404nm. (c) Fluorescence intensities of PN-CDs at different pH values. (d) Fluorescence intensities of PN-CDs after adding various concentrations of NaCl (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400mM) in PBS (pH 7.4). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend,the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of PN-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of ClO− (from top to bottom, 0, 0.06, 0.3, 0.6, 3, 6, 12, 24, 36, 48, 60, 120, 180, 240, 300μM). Inset shows the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and ClO− concentration. (b) Fluorescence spectra of PN-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of ONOO− (from top to bottom, 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10μM). Inset shows the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and ONOO− concentration. (c) Fluorescence spectra of PN-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of NO (from top to bottom, 15, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100μM). Inset shows the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and NO concentration. (d) Reactivity of PN-CDs with various ROS, RNS and reductants. PN-CDs were dissolved in PBS buffer (0.1M, pH 7.4). The concentrations of ROS, RNS and reductants were 300μM.
Confocal fluorescent images of macrophages after the addition of LPS. (a) 0min, (b) 10min, (c) 30min, (d) 50min. (a–d) incubated with 150μg/mL PN-CDs for 24h at 37°C.
Schematic illustration of the construction of the PN-CDs.

head.ce:title: Phosphorus, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for real-time measurement of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species inside macrophages

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cluster Science, Ministry of Education of China, Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Cluster Science, Ministry of Education of China, Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphorus and nitrogen doped carbon dots (PN-CDs) were conveniently prepared by carbonization of adenosine-5′-triphosphate using a hydrothermal treatment. The PN-CDs with P/C atomic ratio of ca. 9.2/100 emit blue luminescence with high quantum yields of up to 23.5%. The PN-CDs were used as a novel sensing platform for live cell imaging of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS), including ClO−, ONOO−, and NO in macrophages. The nanosensor design is based on our new finding that the strong fluorescence of the PN-CDs can be sensitively and selectively quenched by ROS and RNS both in vitro and in vivo. These results reveal that the PN-CDs can serve as a sensitive sensor for rapid imaging of ROS and RNS signaling with high selectivity and contrast.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS)

ce:section.ce:para: Sensitive detection of reactive oxygen and reactive nitrogen species (ROS and RNS) has become the focus of much attention because intracellular ROS/RNS (ClO−, H2O2, 1O2, HO ∙ , ONOO−, NO etc.) concentrations are of primary importance in determining numerous physiological and pathological processes. Recent studies have demonstrated that ROS/RNS play important roles in cell signaling, apoptosis, gene expression, and the activation of cell signaling cascades (D'Autreaux and Toledano, 2007). However, oxidative stress, caused by an imbalance between the generation and detoxification of ROS/RNS, is also implicated in many human diseases, including acute and chronic inflammatory processes, ischemic stroke, ischemia–reperfusion injuries, atherosclerosis, cancer, cardiovascular diseases, and neurodegenerative disorders (Galluzzi et al., 2009; Hansson and Libby, 2006; Seitz and Stickel, 2007; Valko et al., 2007; Winterbourn, 2008). ROS/RNS produced by immune macrophage cells are essential for protecting the human body against invading pathogens (Zhang et al., 2013). The determination of ROS/RNS in macrophages is particularly interesting since it may enable prediction of the response of the immune system to pathogens and cell debris. Due to the high reactivity/instability of ROS/RNS signaling molecules (Woolley et al., 2013), the development of a real-time measurement method for the determination of intracellular ROS/RNS concentration would be highly valuable for determining the nature and identities of the reactive species involved in the phagocytosis process. Fluorescent methods have become effective ways for the detection of ROS/RNS because they allow real time monitoring and visual tracking of ROS/RNS in cells and tissues.

Currently, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) for the detection of ROS/RNS, such as H2O2, (Huang et al., 2013; Rodrigues et al., 2014) HO ∙ , (Wu et al., 2009) NO, (Simoes et al., 2014b), ONOO−(Adegoke and Nyokong, 2013) and so on, (Adegoke and Forbes, 2015) have been prepared through surface modification of QDs. QDs have superior photophysical properties, such as narrow and symmetric emission spectra, large Stokes shifts, and resistance to photobleaching. However, the presence of heavy metals such as cadmium limit their use for in vivo biological applications. Carbon dots (CDs) have been recognized as promising fluorescent materials for bioimaging and biomedical application due to their water-solubility, high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity and good biocompatibility (Lim et al., 2015; Qian et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2015a, 2014). At this time, there are a few CDs probes for fluorescence imaging of cell ROS/RNS levels, including H2O2, (Shan et al., 2014; Wei et al., 2014) HO ∙ , (Ren et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2015b) ClO−(Yin et al., 2013), and ONOO−(Simoes et al., 2014a). However, these CDs probes are produced by various surface modifications (Gao et al., 2014; Kwon et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2013) to introduce recognition sites, and the process is time-consuming and expensive. In addition, the previously reported CDs exhibit low quantum yields (QY) (often less than 20%). (Simoes et al., 2014a; Yin et al., 2013) Therefore, exploring new kinds of CDs with high QY and label-free detection of ROS/RNS is urgently needed.

Doping CDs with heteroatoms like N, P, B, S (Barman et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2014; Kwon et al., 2013; Shen and Xia, 2014; Wang et al., 2014, 2015a; Zhang et al., 2014b) provides an excellent way to effectively tune their intrinsic properties, improve QY, and explore new and interesting phenomena for sensing and bioimaging applications. In this work, the nucleotide adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP) was used as the nitrogen and phosphorus precursor to produce nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (PN-CDs) through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Hydrothermal method is a method that uses biomass and biomass-derived precursors (carbohydrates and etc.) to produce carbonaceous materials under mild conditions. (Rillig et al., 2010) The process takes place via a dehydration–polymerization mechanism to produce carbon particles decorated with polar functional groups, which has been proposed as an ecofriendly and cost-effective process for the fabrication of fluorescent carbon materials (Titirici 2013; Titirici et al., 2007; Zhang et al., 2012). ATP can be synthesized from (aerobic eukaryote) biomass in larger amounts, and thus can serve as a sustainable raw material for the fabrication of functional CDs. The synthetic procedure for PN-CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1. The resulting materials were tested in ROS/RNS in vitro assays and showed rapid fluorescence quenching in the presence of ROS, such as ClO−, and RNS, such as ONOO−/ NO. Moreover, due to the stability over a wide range of pH (4–13) and ionic strength, PN-CDs hold promise for use in bioimaging and biosensing applications.

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and Adenosine (A) were purchased from Adamas-beta. Tert-Butyl hydroperoxide, (TBHP, 70% aq. soln), potassium superoxide, glutathione (GSH) and quinine sulfate were from Alfa Aesar China Ltd. Lipopolysaccharides (LPS) were from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Ascorbic acid and sodium nitrite (NaNO2) were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). A 10% sodium hypochlorite standardized by absorption spectrometry was used for daily preparation of diluted working standards. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Milli-Q ultrapure water (18.2MΩ) was used in each experiment.

In a typical synthesis, ATP was mixed with water (0.1M, 10mL), and the mixture was heated hydrothermally in a stainless steel autoclave at 300°C for 2h. After the resultant light yellow–brown aqueous solution cooled to room temperature, PN-CDs were collected by removing the large particles by filtering through a 0.22µm membrane. The PN-CDs were dialysed against distilled water several times, and subsequently dispersed in water for further characterization and use. In the control experiments, adenosine (A) was mixed with water (0.1M, 10mL); other procedures were the same as those for PN-CDs.

The size and shape of PN-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JSM-1200EX II, JEOL Ltd., Japan and TEM, FEI Tecnai-G2-F20). Infrared spectrometry was conducted on a Nicolet Nexues 670 using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with a PHI 5702 spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα source. Raman spectra were obtained on a Renishaw RW2000 Raman Spectrometer with 514nm wavelength incident laser light. The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a TU-1901 double-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd, Beijing, China). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were acquired on an EPL-405 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, U.K.) with excitation at 404nm.

The QY (Ф) of PN-CDs and N-CDs were determined by a comparative method (Ju et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2014b). As the standard measurement, quinine sulfate (literature QY: 54%) was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (refractive index η=1.33), and PN-CDs were dissolved in distilled water (η=1.33) at different concentrations. The quantum yields of PN-CDs and N-CDs were determined at the excitation wavelength of 340nm by the following equation:
(1)
Φ
x
=
Φ
S T
A
S T
A
x
.
E
x
E
S T
(
η
2
x
η
S T
2
)
where ST and X refer to standard group and test group, respectively; Ф represents the fluorescence quantum yield; A is the absorbance, E is the integrated fluorescence intensity; and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of ROS/RNS specificity and sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para: A 0.1mg/mL PN-CDs aqueous solution in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1M, pH=7.4) was prepared. Then aqueous solutions containing different kinds of ROS/RNS, ClO−, ONOO−, 1O2, HO ∙ , NO, H2O2, O2 −, TBHP, GSH and Vc, were prepared with concentrations of 3mM. To evaluate the specificity of PN-CDs, 0.5mL aliquots of the above solutions were mixed with 4.5mL 150μg/mL PN-CDs solution, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. Since some ROS/RNS exhibit short lifetimes in physiological pH solutions, preparation of fresh ROS/RNS is recommended for analytical uses (Adegoke and Forbes, 2015). The ROS and RNS were prepared as follows: ClO− from NaClO solution; ONOO− from peroxynitrite solution; 1O2 by mixing equal concentrations of H2O2 and NaClO; NO by mixing GSH (0.1844g) and NaNO2 (0.0414g) in 10mL HCl (0.1M) for 30min; O2 − by ionization of potassium superoxide in aqueous solution; HO ∙ by irradiation of H2O2 with UV light. The concentration of NO was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometry using the Lambert–Beer law and the absorbance at 335nm (ε=922M−1 cm−1). All samples were excited at 340nm and the intensities of fluorescence emission at 406nm for each sample were recorded for comparison.

RAW264.7 cells were chosen to demonstrate the feasibility of PN-CDs for cell imaging. RAW264.7 cells were seeded in a 12-well culture plate at a density of 2×104 per well in Dulbecco modified Eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1.5g/L sodium bicarbonate, penicillin (100units/mL) and streptomycin (100units/mL) at 37°C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24h. After removing DMEM, PN-CDs solutions were mixed with DMEM (0.15mg/mL) and added to the cell culture, and the cells were incubated for another 24h. After the cells were washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10mM, pH=7.4), fluorescence imaging was conducted by a fluorescence microscope (Olympus FV1000-LX81 microscope) for 50min. To generate intracellular ROS/RNS, lipopolysaccharides (LPS) were utilized to induce oxygen bursts in macrophage cells (Blonska et al., 2003; Laskin and Pendino 1995; MacMicking et al., 1995). Real time confocal imaging was performed for 50min. Macrophage cells were first observed for 5min, and then LPS was added to the medium at a final concentration of 1μg/mL. The cells were continuously monitored for an additional 45min to evaluate intracellular ROS/RNS generation caused by LPS stimulation.

The redox properties of PN-CDs were examined by cyclic voltammetry using a standard three-electrode system, consisting of a gold sheet as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The electrolyte solution was 0.1M (Bu)4NBF4 in dried toluene (Zhang et al., 2014b).

In vitro cytotoxicity of the PN-CDs was assessed by the standard MTT assay. In brief, 100μL of RAW264.7 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate with a density of 5×103 cells/mL for 12h. Culture medium was discarded and cells were then treated with DMEM containing various concentrations of PN-CDs (25–250μg/mL) for another 24h. At the end of the incubation, the culture medium was removed and 10μL MTT (5.0mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well. After additional 3h incubation, the growth medium was removed and 100μL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve MTT. Finally, the optical density of each sample was recorded using a microplate reader (Bio-rad680, USA) at a wavelength of 570nm.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to investigate the morphology of PN-CDs ( Fig. 1a and b), which can be seen to have a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation, and particle diameter of 1.2–2.0nm with an average size of 1.8nm (Figure S1). From the high-resolution TEM, PN-CDs are crystalline with a lattice spacing of 0.27nm, which agrees with the spacing of the (020) planes of graphitic carbons (Chen et al., 2015). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicates that PN-CDs are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen (Fig. 1c and Table S1) (Ding et al., 2014; Vedamalai et al., 2014). The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows four peaks centered at 284.7, 285.3, 286.2, and 288.0eV, which are attributed to C–C, C–P, C–N, and C=O, respectively (Ju and Chen 2014). The high resolution N1s spectrum (Figure S2a) reveals the presence of pyridinic N (399.2eV), pyrrolic N (400.1eV), and N–H (401.1eV) (Zeng et al., 2015). The P2p spectrum (Figure S2b) shows the presence of P–O (133.2eV) and P–C (134.0eV). In the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Figure S3), the following are observed: heterocyclic aromatic CN at 1430–1650cm−1 and N–H at 3370cm−1, O–H stretching bands at 3150cm−1, P–O at 1113cm−1 (Du et al., 2014). Furthermore, the ζ-potential of PN-CDs is −14.7mV, confirming the presence of negative functional groups (such as carboxyl groups) on the surface of PN-CDs. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was also used to characterize the PN-CDs (Figure S4). PN-CDs show a broad (002) peak at around 24.3°, which further confirms the graphene structure of PN-CDs (Tang et al., 2012). Raman spectra of PN-CDs (Figure S5) show two peaks centered at 1370cm−1 (D band) and 1590cm−1 (G band), respectively. The intensity ratio ID /IG is 0.68, indicating good quality (Wang et al., 2014).

In UV–vis spectra ( Fig. 2a), the PN-CDs exhibit three typical absorption peaks around 240nm, 280nm and 310nm. The peak at 240nm corresponds to the
π →
π
*
transition of the aromatic sp2 domains (Zhang and Chen 2014). The peak at 280nm is assigned to the
n →
π
*
transition of C=O. The absorption peak at 310nm is due to the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states (Zhang et al., 2014a). The aqueous solution of PN-CDs is light brown and shows bright blue luminescence under 365nm UV light (inset of Fig. 2a). Similar to observations in previous reports, (Jin et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2013) PN-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, as displayed in Fig. 2a. The origin of the phenomenon could be attributed to the effect of surface states on the band gap of CDs. The surface states are similar to molecular states, which contribute to the complexity of the excited states of CDs (Bao et al., 2011; Dong et al., 2013; Li et al., 2012; Tang et al., 2012; Zheng et al., 2011). The QY of PN-CDs excited with 340nm was as high as 23.5%. The time decay of PN-CDs is displayed in Fig. 2b. The fluorescence lifetime of PN-CDs was fitted by a multi-exponential function, giving two decay times, τ 1=1.64ns (35.7%) and τ 2=6.50ns (64.3%), with an average lifetime of 4.76ns (Wang et al., 2015b). Moreover, a control synthesis experiment was performed to test the luminescence mechanism of carbon dots (Figure S6-8). Changes of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and bandgap energies of CDs contribute to the fluorescence properties (Song et al., 2014). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to estimate the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of PN-CDs (Figure S9). In addition, Fig. 2c and d shows that the fluorescence intensities of PN-CDs are strong and stable over a wide range of pH values (pH 4–13) and high ionic concentrations (up to 400mM NaCl).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro studies of PN-CDs for ROS and RNS detection
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of PN-CDs as a nanosensor for the detection of different biologically relevant ROS/RNS was investigated. Fig. 3a shows the fluorescence spectra of PN-CDs after adding various concentrations of ClO−. Upon the addition of ClO−, a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed. An excellent linear correlation (R 2=0.9938) was observed for the dependence of the quenching effects, (F 0−F)/F 0, on the concentration over the range of 5–200μM. The reaction could be completed in 1min (Figure S10), suggesting that the method is promising for real-time detection of ROS/RNS. Moreover, the influence of pH on the quenching response of PN-CDs towards ClO− was also evaluated, and the greatest effect was observed in the pH range of 7–9 (Figure S11), with a maximum effect at pH 7.4.

Similar phenomena were observed for the addition of ONOO− (Fig. 3b) and NO (Fig. 3c). These results show a linear relationship between (F 0−F)/F 0 and ROS/RNS concentration over a wide dynamic range (R 2=0.9911 from 1–60μM for ONOO−, and 0.9990 from 10–100μM for NO). The limits of detection (calculated according to a signal-to-noise ratio of S/N=3) for ClO−, ONOO− and NO were found to be 0.28μM, 0.38μM, and 1.10μM, respectively.

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is attributed to the oxidation of PN-CDs. After the addition of ClO-, the quenched PN-CDs showed two decay times, τ 1=0.15ns (85.57%) and τ 2=4.68ns (14.43%) (Figure S12), with an average lifetime of 0.80ns. The decay time decreased from 4.76ns (unquenched) to 0.80ns (quenched). Thus, we propose that the quenching of fluorescence due to ClO− resulted from surface changes of PN-CDs caused by the oxidation of the surface groups with ClO−. UV–vis spectra of PN-CDs before and after being oxidized were also collected. Compared to the original PN-CDs, the intensity of the 240nm peak of the oxidized PN-CDs obviously increased, also proving the change of the surface states of PN-CDs.

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3d, some ROS/RNS, such as 1O2 and HO ∙ commonly present in biological matrices also led to 50% fluorescence quenching of PN-CDs, while other ROS/RNS (H2O2, O2 −, TBHP) had little quenching effect. This could be explained by the fact the highly reactive oxygen species (hROS) have a stronger oxidation tendency than other ROS (Table S1). According to the cyclic voltammogram of PN-CDs (Figure S13), PN-CDs can be oxidized by oxidants whose reduction potentials are less negative than −0.9V. Also, quenching should be more efficient for oxidants with higher reduction potential. Thus, the quench efficiency is ClO-> ONOO-> NO. 1O2 is a strong oxidant and displays considerable reactivity, but the lifetime of 1O2 is extremely short (10−6 s). Similarly, HO ∙ has a short lifetime of 10− 9 s. The short lifetimes affect the scavenge effect of PN-CDs. H2O2 has a higher reduction potential than ClO− and ONOO−, but reactions of H2O2 have higher activation energies and slower rates, making quenching by H2O2 rare (Nova Science Publishers, 2011; Davies et al., 2008; Winterbourn, 2008). Furthermore, the reactivity of O2 − with nonradical targets is limited, resulting in very little influence on the fluorescence of PN-CDs. Moreover, reducing agents such as GSH and Vc have no obvious effect on the fluorescence emission of PN-CDs.
Above all, the low LODs for ROS/RNS make PN-CDs feasible for both in vitro and in vivo imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live cell imaging of ROS and RNS with PN-CDs in macrophages
ce:section.ce:para: After demonstrating the vitro response characteristics of the PN-CD nanosensor, we then explored the feasibility of PN-CDs for live cell imaging of ROS and RNS signaling. We first used macrophages as the model system. As shown in Fig. 4, by incubating the macrophages with PN-CDs for 24h at 37°C, a significant blue emission from within the cell was observed. After incubating the macrophages with PN-CDs for 50min, the fluorescence intensities remained almost unchanged (Figure S14). To further investigate if PN-CDs can be applied for monitoring of the intracellular ROS/RNS, LPS was introduced into macrophages. When macrophages are exploded by LPS, which is an endotoxin from Gram-negative bacteria, macrophages actively participate in inflammatory response by secretion of inflammatory mediators such as ROS and RNS (Blonska et al., 2003; Laskin and Pendino 1995; MacMicking et al., 1995). Addition of LPS is a common model employed in studies of inflammatory disorders caused by mismanaged generation of ROS and RNS (Adegoke and Forbes 2015; Yang et al., 2011). In Fig. 4, 30min after the addition of LPS, the fluorescence intensity of PN-CDs decreased significantly. After 50min (Fig. 4e), the microscope images showed very weak fluorescence, indicating that PN-CDs could be applied for imaging intracellular ROS/RNS. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of PN-CDs was negligible, as confirmed by the standard MTT assay, as shown in Fig. S15. All these results indicate that PN-CDs can serve as an effective intracellular ROS/RNS imaging agent.

In summary, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots have been easily synthesized from the nitrogen and phosphorus rich precursor of ATP using a simple, low-cost and environmental-friendly strategy. The PN-CDs have a high quantum yield of 23.5%, and are remarkably stable against extreme pH and high ionic strengths, making them useful for applications in physiological conditions. PN-CDs can be applied for label-free, sensitive and real-time detection of different kinds of ROS/RNS. PN-CDs also have the remarkable advantages of high sensitivity, rapid response time, wide response range, and high biocompatibility, thus making them suitable for in vivo ROS/RNS detection and biological imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21275018 and 21203008), Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS) (20140121), Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (RFDP) (No. 20121101110049) and the 111 Project (B07012) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.01.022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable Carbon Materials From Hydrothermal Processes, 1,
